perturbation.original_text: 7. Buyer will not, for a period of three (3) years from the date hereof, enter into any agreement for the purchase of the Business, in whole or in part, or assist or promote any other party in so doing, unless such agreement to purchase provides for commission to be paid Broker, with the commission being defined as the amount agreed upon by Broker and Seller in the "Business Listing Contract”, “Engagement Agreement” or similar agreement between those parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Buyer will not, for a period of ten (10) years from the date hereof, enter into any agreement for the purchase of the Business, in whole or in part, or assist or promote any other party in so doing, unless such agreement to purchase provides for commission to be paid Broker, with the commission being defined as the amount agreed upon by Broker and Seller in the "Business Listing Contract”, “Engagement Agreement” or similar agreement between those parties.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to ten years may be deemed unreasonably long under Pennsylvania law, potentially making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania courts scrutinize non-compete agreements for reasonableness, considering factors like duration and geographic scope. A ten-year restriction, without clear justification related to the protection of legitimate business interests (trade secrets, customer relationships), may be considered excessive and therefore unenforceable under Pennsylvania law. The law requires that any noncompete is reasonable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from three years to ten years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that under Pennsylvania law, such an extended period, without clear justification, may be considered unreasonable and therefore unenforceable. This creates a legal contradiction because the extended duration directly challenges the requirement of reasonableness in non-compete agreements under Pennsylvania law, even though no specific snippets were provided to define the specific length of time that is seen as reasonable. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition fits because a time-sensitive obligation (the length of the non-compete) is potentially violating state law, making the contractual term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be arbitrated in Bucks County, Pennsylvania on written request of one (1) party served on the other. Such arbitration shall be under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitrator shall have no authority to change any provisions of this agreement; the arbitrator’s sole authority shall be to interpret or apply the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be arbitrated in secret in a jurisdiction of the Broker’s choice on written request of one (1) party served on the other. Such arbitration shall be under the rules of the Broker’s choosing. The arbitrator shall have no authority to change any provisions of this agreement; the arbitrator’s sole authority shall be to interpret or apply the provisions of this Agreement. Discovery will be severely limited to only that which is specifically allowed by the Broker.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting discovery and changing arbitration rules to favor the broker could render the arbitration clause unconscionable and unenforceable, violating principles of fair arbitration under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania's Uniform Arbitration Act implies a standard of fairness and impartiality in arbitration proceedings. Severely limiting discovery and giving the Broker unilateral control over the arbitration process could be viewed as undermining the fairness of the process, making the clause potentially unenforceable. Courts can refuse to enforce arbitration agreements that are unconscionable or procured unfairly.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces elements that severely limit fairness and impartiality in arbitration, potentially rendering the clause unconscionable under Pennsylvania law. Specifically, it changes the arbitration location to a jurisdiction of the Broker’s choice, allows the Broker to choose the arbitration rules, and severely limits discovery to only what the Broker allows. While the provided snippet only names the Act, the Law Explanation explicitly states Pennsylvania's Uniform Arbitration Act implies a standard of fairness and impartiality in arbitration proceedings. This alteration undermines this implied standard, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Buyer will indemnify and hold harmless the Broker and Seller from any and all claims or actions arising from Buyer's acts or failures to act in pursuing the possible purchase of the Business, including, without limitation, reasonable attorney's fees and other expenses incurred by Broker and seller.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Buyer will indemnify and hold harmless the Broker and Seller from any and all claims or actions, including claims of gross negligence or willful misconduct by the Broker or Seller, arising from Buyer's acts or failures to act in pursuing the possible purchase of the Business, including, without limitation, attorney's fees and other expenses incurred by Broker and seller.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the buyer to indemnify the Broker and Seller even for their own gross negligence or willful misconduct is against public policy and may be unenforceable in Pennsylvania.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on indemnification agreements and public policy
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania law prohibits indemnity for intentional misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law generally disallows indemnification for one's own intentional or reckless conduct. While an agreement to indemnify another party for their negligence may be permissible under certain conditions, a contractual provision requiring indemnification for gross negligence or willful misconduct is likely to be deemed void as against public policy. The specific case supports the general principle disallowing indemnity for intentional misconduct.
perturbation.justification: The modified text requires the buyer to indemnify the Broker and Seller even for their own gross negligence or willful misconduct. The Law Explanation states that Pennsylvania law generally disallows indemnification for one's own intentional or reckless conduct, and that a contractual provision requiring indemnification for gross negligence or willful misconduct is likely to be deemed void as against public policy. Since the changed text explicitly includes 'gross negligence or willful misconduct', it contradicts the principle of not allowing indemnification for one's own intentional or reckless conduct as described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 9. The performance and construction of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania. All sums due hereunder shall be payable at the office of the Broker in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts in and for Bucks County, Pennsylvania to enforce the terms of this Agreement. Claims for injunctive relief may be filed in any court with appropriate jurisdiction over the matter. All other claims must be arbitrated in accordance with section 11 below.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The performance and construction of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Broker's choice, regardless of conflict of laws principles. All sums due hereunder shall be payable at the office of the Broker in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction of a court of the Broker's choosing. Claims for injunctive relief may be filed in any court with appropriate jurisdiction over the matter. All other claims must be arbitrated in accordance with section 11 below.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the Broker to unilaterally choose the governing law and jurisdiction, irrespective of conflict of law principles or the location of the buyer/seller, could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on choice of law and forum selection clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Pennsylvania courts generally respect choice of law provisions, they will not enforce them if the chosen law violates a fundamental public policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the litigation. The Restatement and cases interpreting it show that a state's law should be chosen with consideration for the location of involved parties and the issues at hand, rather than by arbitrary selection. Allowing the Broker unlimited choice of law and jurisdiction could be seen as unfair and against public policy, making the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Pennsylvania law governs the agreement and sets exclusive jurisdiction in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. The changed text allows the Broker to choose the governing law and jurisdiction, disregarding conflict of laws principles. This contradicts the principle that choice of law should consider the involved parties and issues, potentially violating public policy if the Broker chooses a law unfairly advantageous to them, as elaborated in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable under Pennsylvania law. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the change in the contract language combined with the Law and Perturbation Explanations creates a legal contradiction.
